summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' is nearing its final draft and will be produced as an audiobook by Random House. She reflects on the long, uncertain journey of writing the book and the relief of finally seeing tangible progress. The post captures the emotional arc of completing a major project—from the disorienting uncertainty of research to the satisfaction of a finished product. She expresses enthusiasm about potentially narrating the audiobook herself, having developed an appreciation for how the book sounds read aloud.
key_points: The book is finally approaching completion after a long period of uncertainty about whether it would come together
Random House has agreed to produce an audiobook version
O'Neil enlisted friends to read chapters aloud to her as part of the editing process
She reflects that she might not have started the project if she'd known how difficult and uncertain it would be
She's campaigning to be chosen as the audiobook narrator
money_quotes: I likely wouldn't have taken it on at all if I'd actually known how big and how long, and how uncertain I'd be, so often, that it would end up being a book.
So, I guess, here's to running into things with almost pure ignorance!

What's cool about this phase is, first of all it seems real, and countable, like you can feel the progress happening, whereas so many other moments seemed completely lost in time, swallowed up by a very loud hum of uncertainty.
Now, at least, I know there's a book here.
This is happening, people! Fuck yes!
key_insight: Major creative projects often succeed precisely because we don't fully understand their scope when we begin—pure ignorance can be a gift that keeps us from being paralyzed by the enormity of the task.
